title: Rocket Report: Rebuild begins at Blue Origin launch pad; Relativity targets Mars
gist: Ars Technica's Rocket Report roundup covers SpaceX Starship Flight 13 timing, repeated Isar Aerospace scrubs, the Chinese Zhuque-2E upper-stage breakup creating debris near the ISS and Starlink, Relativity's 2028 Mars orbiter, Blue Origin's New Glenn pad rebuild, and Amazon's launch-vehicle dependency on Ariane 6.

insights.claim: SpaceX's next Starship test (Flight 13) could launch as soon as next month per COO Gwynne Shotwell, repeating the prior suborbital profile with an Indian Ocean splashdown; an orbital flight is held off until at least Flight 14 after the ship failed a critical in-space engine restart on the last flight.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Starship orbital capability is slipping by at least one flight; a milestone watch item for SpaceX's roadmap and Artemis dependencies.

insights.claim: Isar Aerospace, despite raising nearly $1 billion, has scrubbed its second Spectrum test flight four times in five months (pressurization valve, fuel temperatures, leak, fluid-system anomaly) and has only one prior flight — a failure under 30 seconds.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Capital does not substitute for flight experience; Europe's leading rocket startup remains far from operational.
insights.quote: Isar is flush with cash, having raised nearly $1 billion to date, but is still lacking in the critical currency of flight experience.

insights.claim: French startup Latitude removed all 'Zephyr' branding from its rocket, plausibly due to trademark conflict with Airbus subsidiary AALTO's Zephyr HAPS aircraft, and is targeting H2 2027 for its 200kg-to-LEO launcher's first flight.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Minor IP/branding risk note; another European small-launch entrant years from flight.

insights.claim: A Chinese LandSpace Zhuque-2E upper stage broke apart on June 9 in a heavily trafficked orbit (208-263 miles) near the ISS and Starlink direct-to-cell satellites; the Space Force tracks at least 51 objects, with LeoLabs estimating 100-150 debris pieces.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Adds collision risk in a critical orbital band; reinforces demand for space-domain-awareness and debris-tracking services.
insights.locator: China's Zhuque-2E breaks up in orbit

insights.claim: Relativity Space announced an Interplanetary Sciences Program with a first Mars science/telecom orbiter targeted for late 2028 on its Terran R rocket, reflecting CEO Eric Schmidt's push into orbital data centers, philanthropic space science, and national security missions.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Relativity is broadening beyond launch under Schmidt; the Mars claim is light on details and echoes a previously unrealized 2022 plan.

insights.claim: Blue Origin has cleared debris and begun rebuilding Launch Complex 36 after a New Glenn exploded there during a preflight test last month, and Bezos/Limp reiterate New Glenn will fly again before year-end with the Blue Moon Mark 1 lunar lander now targeted for early next year.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Aggressive return-to-flight timeline that NASA officials doubt; a key dependency for Artemis lunar-lander testing.
insights.locator: Rebuild underway at Blue Origin's Florida launch pad

insights.claim: A new Cape Canaveral pad (Launch Complex 51) is being explored for Navy and Army missions to replace LC-46, which cannot operate simultaneously with Blue Origin's adjacent LC-36 due to its explosive clear zone.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Spaceport congestion is driving new infrastructure; relevant to launch-cadence capacity at the Cape.
insights.locator: New launch pad in the works at Cape Canaveral

insights.claim: Amazon has hundreds of flight-ready Leo satellites sitting idle in Florida; of its three chosen unproven rockets (ULA Vulcan, Blue Origin New Glenn, Ariane 6), only Ariane 6 has delivered, with Arianespace's Ariane 64 setting Europe's heaviest-payload record carrying 36 Amazon Leo satellites.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Launch-vehicle scarcity is the binding constraint on Amazon's constellation buildout; Arianespace is the unexpected critical partner.
insights.quote: They're built and sitting in a payload processing facility waiting for trips to orbit
insights.locator: For Amazon, Europe's Ariane 6 stands apart

insights.claim: AST SpaceMobile launched only three BlueBird satellites this week on a Falcon 9 after New Glenn's grounding from its April explosion, reaching mid-2026 at just three new satellites versus a 45-satellite annual goal.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Launch dependency severely throttles AST SpaceMobile's deployment vs. plan; a material execution risk for the Starlink direct-to-cell competitor.
insights.locator: SpaceX gives a ride to a competitor

insights.claim: Japan's H3 returned to flight June 12 with a new all-liquid H3-30S variant (third hydrogen engine, no solid boosters) for lower-cost medium-satellite launches, recovering from a December failure caused by payload-support-structure collapse.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Japan restores sovereign launch capability with a cheaper variant, adding medium-lift competition.
insights.locator: Japan's H3 rocket returns to flight
